How to maximize your career impact with Tobi Lutke | Rob Norris posted on the topic | LinkedIn

````````````[Skip to main content](#main-content)

[![View profile for Rob Norris]()](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/robjnorris?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Rob Norris](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/robjnorris?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
SVP Product @ Nasdaq Verafin
7moEdited
Just sharing this episode of Lenny's Product Management Podcast with Tobi Lutke as I thought it was a gem. I listen to podcasts when I drive to/from the office - I listened to this one twice.
One of the great, sobering insights from this interview with Shopify's CEO is simply and plainly stating that our entire working careers aren't actually that long. Your career will be 30-40 years - and that's if you're lucky.
"You want to do the maximum amount of things you can be proud of at the end of your career."
As I've been getting older (approximately half way through Tobi's time range) the idea has also struck me on maximizing impact in the time you have left. Gotta sieze each and every day to move things along. Gotta build - Gotta ship - Gotta sell - Gotta continuously raise the bar. This also means stopping and culling the wrong things that are consuming the clock. Time is ticking and doing the wrong thing is burning time. When stepping back and reminding yourself of this: "compressing time" becomes a very useful tool.
All of the above applies to life as well as your work.

Many leaders face a hidden hurdle: the fear of success. In this insightful episode of Your Morning Boost, Dr. Albert Bramante uncovers how this fear, rooted in an apprehension of change and new responsibilities, can lead to self-sabotaging behaviors. The discussion provides critical takeaways on recognizing these fears, embracing discomfort, and pushing through to achieve personal and professional growth. 🔥Empathy Isn’t Enough for Great Leadership
We’ve been told for years that empathy is the ultimate leadership superpower.
And it is important —it connects us, it builds trust, it humanizes the workplace.
But here’s the truth I’ve learned: empathy alone can break a leader.
Unchecked empathy can:
Delay hard decisions
Create burnout
Blur boundaries
Even erode respect
That’s where compassion comes in.
Compassion is empathy with direction.
It’s the ability to understand someone’s experience and act in a way that serves both them and the greater good.
It’s what allows leaders to make tough calls with dignity, clarity, and humanity.
In the latest episode of The H2H Experiment, I explore:
💡Why empathy without boundaries can harm leaders and organizations
💡How compassion transforms leadership decisions
💡Practical ways to develop compassion as a skill
💡An HX Experiment you can try this week to move from empathy to compassion
Because empathy connects us…
…but compassion moves us forward.
🎧Listen to the full episode here:[https://lnkd.in/ddHtzbbE](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/ddHtzbbE&urlhash=p9Gx&trk=public_post-text)[#TheH2HExperiment](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/theh2hexperiment&trk=public_post-text)[#LeadershipDevelopment](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadershipdevelopment&trk=public_post-text)[#CompassionateLeadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/compassionateleadership&trk=public_post-text)[#EmpathyInLeadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/empathyinleadership&trk=public_post-text)[#HumanExperience](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/humanexperience&trk=public_post-text)[#FutureOfWork](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/futureofwork&trk=public_post-text)[#EmotionalIntelligence](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/emotionalintelligence&trk=public_post-text)

Be a leader who finishes!
1. Pre-decision > Re-decision (proactive priority scheduling > reactive re-scheduling)
2. Design Triggers > Setting Goals (atomic habit patterns; when > what)
3. Measure > Burnout (momentum through progress tracking)
4. Why > What (purpose and passion drives us during tough times)
Consistently 1% better per day/week for 5 years = 12x better!

When you’re making a big decision, do you trust a popular voice or the person who’s actually done the work? Most buyers choose the expert. 😜In the new episode of The ROIght Marketing Podcast, I share how to show your expertise without feeling salesy, and how that kind of credibility moves decisions.
✅A simple definition of thought leadership (vs. generic content)

[![View profile for Russell Fradin]()](https://www.linkedin.com/in/rfradin?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

5mo
By now, you’ve probably seen the leaked memo from[Shopify](https://ca.linkedin.com/company/shopify?trk=public_post-text)CEO[Tobias Lütke](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/tobiaslutke?trk=public_post-text). He couldn’t spell it out any clearer: the modern enterprise has completely changed.
What struck me wasn't just his mandate that "using AI effectively is now a fundamental expectation"…it was the underlying challenge every executive now faces.
What Tobi's really saying is: don't ask for more engineers until all of your engineers are using a tool such as Cursor. Show me you're maximizing your current resources before asking for more. And he's right.
The modern executive needs to answer these 3 key questions:
- What actual output am I getting from current resources?
- When should I invest in more AI tools vs. adding headcount?
- How do I find the optimal mix of human and AI capabilities?
This isn't about replacing humans…far from it. It's about understanding the complex dynamic between humans and AI and what truly drives productivity in your organization. Especially in a rough economy where you can’t out-hire problems.
For CFOs getting annual headcount requests without visibility into team productivity, it's like being asked to write blank checks.
The companies that will excel aren't those mandating AI adoption through executive decree –they'll be the ones that can measure, understand, and optimize how humans and AI work together.
This is exactly why we built[Larridin](https://www.linkedin.com/company/larridin?trk=public_post-text). The future isn't all AI or all human. It's finding the right balance –and you can't manage what you can't measure.

Mid-market companies investing in AI solutions that do not include robust data integration and processing are setting themselves up for a ton of frustration. Insights never go far enough when the Shopify inventory data isn't linked to cash balances and working capital. You can’t get deep financial analysis if your AI agent can’t read journal entries. This foundational problem has shaped Payflow’s product development for the last six months. We've focused on building an integrated data layer that normalizes structured and unstructured financial data, before applying any AI logic. Key benefits of our unified approach: •Eliminates data silos that limit analytical depth •Enables multi-dimensional analysis across customer acquisition costs, operational metrics, and financial outcomes •Bridges seemingly disparate data points
•Surfaces insights invisible in traditional reporting structures True AI transformation in finance requires partners who are solving the integration and modeling problems in addition to delivering powerful agents. Companies that recognize this distinction will gain a competitive advantage in building internally or selecting the right vendors while others wonder why their AI “isn’t working.”

👉What AI projects have you seen deliver real results in small business?
Continuing from my last post on MIT’s finding that 95% of AI projects don’t actually boost revenue…
For small businesses, the bar is even higher —every dollar has to count. When applied to the right problems, AI can absolutely move the needle. A University of St Andrews study of nearly 10,000 SMEs (Small and Medium-sized Enterprise) reported productivity gains of 27% to 133%, with the biggest benefits coming in scheduling, marketing, and inventory management.
Here are a few ideas that seem to be more profitable than others:
1️⃣ AI-generated marketing copy & social posts
Save hours each week on drafting content. Many small businesses are already doing this, and the time saved means more visibility with less effort.
2️⃣ Customer service chatbots for FAQs
Handle the repetitive stuff so your team can focus on the real issues. Even simple bots can cover 30–70% of incoming questions, which keeps customers happy and staff costs down.
3️⃣ Sales forecasting models
A little more accuracy here goes a long way. Better forecasts mean fewer stockouts, less waste, and smoother cash flow.
4️⃣ Staff scheduling optimization
The study specifically called out rostering as a huge win. Smarter schedules cut down overtime and make better use of your team’s time.
5️⃣ Inventory and demand prediction
Another big one from the study —tighter control of stock reduces waste and frees up cash that would otherwise sit on the shelf.
The takeaway: start small, pick one pain point, and make sure the results show up in your revenue or productivity before scaling.[https://lnkd.in/e5EScbNu](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/e5EScbNu&urlhash=yb8s&trk=public_post-text)

[![View profile for Josh Pineda]()](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/hjoshpineda?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

Founder @ OneNine | Simplify workflows, reduce manual tasks, and scale your team’s impact | Agentic AI
1mo
If you're leading a tech company and your CFO asked where AI is improving margins, not just generating headlines, what would you show?
Most tech companies can’t show how AI is improving margins.
In the past few weeks, I’ve spoken with dozens of tech leaders at scaling companies. Many are building AI features for users. Not always because it’s the right move, but because investors are asking for it or because everyone else is doing it.
Meanwhile, internal operations are still struggling:
•Approvals are manual.
•Data is fragmented.
•Teams rely on duct-taped tools.
That’s the real AI paradox.
Do we build AI into the product, or use it to strengthen the foundation of the business?
There are three ways companies get ROI from AI:
1. Operational efficiency - Cut costs by reducing friction and manual work.
2. Customer acquisition - Launch attention-grabbing AI features that drive growth.
3. Customer retention - Build tools that improve user experience, personalization, and support.
You’ll eventually need all three. But in tech, skipping ops is the most common and the most costly mistake.
Look at who started with operations.
➡️Walmart focused on backend AI in their supply chain.
Their automated warehouses now process twice the volume with half the staff.
That unlocked 130 billion dollars in sales growth over five years without increasing headcount.
➡️Salesforce cleaned its data before anything else.
They merged 266 million disconnected profiles into 141 million real ones.
This led to:
•60 percent lift in lead revenue.
•40 percent boost in pipeline efficiency.
•23 million dollars in proactive renewals.
•85 percent of support tasks now handled by AI.
None of that came from launching a new AI product.
It came from fixing what was slowing them down.
---
This is where agentic AI comes in.
It’s not just automation.
It’s autonomous agents that:
•Watch your internal systems.
•Make smart decisions.
•Trigger workflows.
•Learn and improve over time.
Agentic AI turns fragmented operations into high-leverage systems.
If your AI strategy doesn’t start with operations, you’re skipping the highest-leverage path to scale.
Fix what’s slowing you down.
Then scale what’s working.

[![View profile for Slawomir Sawicki]()](https://pl.linkedin.com/in/slawomir-sawicki/en?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Slawomir Sawicki](https://pl.linkedin.com/in/slawomir-sawicki/en?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
Engineer with a business skills | Background in AI and Data
1mo
AI in e-commerce is no longer a “trend” –it’s reality.
4 out of 5 businesses are already using it, often without even noticing.
Generative AI writes product descriptions, emails, and ads.
Computer vision looks at photos and shows the customer “something similar.”
Predictive analytics? The fortune teller of sales –it knows what will sell fast and what will just gather dust.
The store begins to think for itself.
It restocks automatically, changes prices dynamically, chats with customers 24/7.
The owner sleeps, while revenue keeps rolling in.
Sounds like a dream?
It’s more like the new normal –and Shopify says it’s easier than most think.
No PhD, no NASA-sized budget required.
But here’s the catch: in all of this, the human gets blurred.
If the store is autonomous –then who is the merchant?
A bot administrator? An algorithm supervisor?
Yes, AI gives you an edge.
But every edge comes with a question of meaning: are we building businesses, or just feeding machines?
For now –instead of philosophizing –the practical move is simple.
Pick one AI use case: product copy, a chatbot, or dynamic pricing.
Test it. Measure the effect.
The bigger reflection can wait: is this store still “mine” –or is it already “ours,” shared between me and the algorithm?

They removed search altogether.

"site:help.shopify.com"
And I'm hoping this won't become common practice on help pages.
We're not trying to write an essay.

Where’s the biggest AI opportunity? 🤔Low Digital / High Adoption Potential 🚀vs High Digital / High Adoption Potential ⚡1️⃣ Low Digital / High Adoption Potential –“Leapfrog Markets”
💡AI here can deliver step change gains —taking businesses from manual →predictive in one move.
💰Often more lucrative for new players because the wins are obvious and immediate.
Examples:
Twiga Foods →AI for agricultural supply chain forecasting.
Wasoko →AI for retail inventory & demand planning.
Sendy (SMB logistics fleets) →AI for routing & load matching.
Local healthcare clinics →AI for patient scheduling, billing, and claims automation.
Why attractive:
Less entrenched tech = easier adoption.
ROI is visible quickly.
You’re building the machine, not just tuning it.
2️⃣ High Digital / High Adoption Potential –“Optimisation Markets”
💡AI here still has opportunity —but it’s about fine-tuning a well-oiled machine.
💰Harder for outsiders to break in, many big players already have in-house AI teams and budgets.
Examples:
Amazon →AI for logistics fine-tuning & product recommendations.
Maersk →AI for supply chain optimisation & predictive ETA.
Goldman Sachs →AI for risk modelling & fraud detection.
Shopify →AI for personalised storefronts & marketing optimisation.
Why harder for outsiders:
Most “big wins” already solved.
Long procurement cycles.
Ideas must be highly differentiated to get attention.
The takeaway:
Big companies aren’t “done” with AI.
But the opportunities that may occur are not from the established players who have long led the tech market.
Based on this “vs”, if a group or even a few people implement AI into certain low-digital companies… those businesses could take massive leaps. 🚀True or false?

Instead of waiting on IT or hiring developers, any employee can now build custom AI agents using a simple drag-and-drop interface. Think of it as giving your team superpowers to automate repetitive tasks, answer customer questions, or pull insights from company data.
Why this is significant:
→It democratizes AI. Anyone can create solutions for their specific pain points.
→Security is baked in. It connects to your internal data and tools with enterprise-like controls, so you're not sacrificing protection for convenience.
→You don't start from scratch. Google included prebuilt agents for common tasks like research and customer support.
The business case:
Starting at $21-30/user/month, this positions Google squarely against Microsoft and Amazon in the enterprise AI race. For companies looking to scale AI adoption without massive IT investments.
Bottom line: How quickly you can empower your people to use it.

2mo
We decided to start using AI tools long before we began offering AI services. I felt we needed to lead by example. We had to understand what AI is and how it can be used before making recommendations to others. We use AI tools internally to make ourselves more efficient, to refine how we work, how to present ourselves, and how to consolidate our knowledge so we can distill the best pieces to offer. We also use these tools to organize information in a way that helps us move forward more effectively.
I read an article where the CEO of Shopify said that moving forward, if someone wanted to hire a new employee, they would need to justify that by showing the job couldn’t be done through AI. I really liked that perspective. I want my team to use AI responsibly but without fear, to see it as a benefit for our company and our clients.
I’m not saying AI does our work. What I’m saying is that it helps us shape things better and identify our blind spots, because we all have them. And since these tools are objective, they can point out those blind spots so we can transform and improve. That’s a theme for us: to be better, both for each other and for our clients.

The Great[#AI](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/ai&trk=public_post-text)Irony
In the last few days, I reached out to customer support across some of the biggest technology companies in the world.[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post-text),[Google](https://www.linkedin.com/company/google?trk=public_post-text)My Business,[GSuite](https://in.linkedin.com/company/gsuiteapps?trk=public_post-text).[Microsoft 365](https://www.linkedin.com/showcase/microsoft-365/?trk=public_post-text),[Meta](https://www.linkedin.com/company/meta?trk=public_post-text),[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/linkedin?trk=public_post-text)-Recruiter. Each of these companies proudly claims to be shaping the future of Artificial Intelligence.
They sell AI as the ultimate solution to make businesses smarter, faster, and more efficient. But when it comes to their own systems, that intelligence is missing. Their customer support chatbots cannot understand context. They cannot solve basic problems. They repeat scripted answers and loop users into endless automated replies.
It feels less like talking to intelligence, and more like arguing with a wall. The irony is glaring.
The very companies preaching AI transformation are struggling to use it within their own ecosystems. If the creators of AI cannot deploy it effectively to assist their customers, what exactly are they selling to us?
What we are witnessing is not intelligence. It is automation with good PR. A simulation of understanding, without any actual empathy or problem solving capability.
For MSME owners and small businesses who are constantly told that AI will revolutionize their operations, this is a wake up call. If these billion dollar giants cannot use AI to fix their own customer experience, maybe the technology is not ready to run your business yet. AI will not replace humans.
But it seems to have already replaced accountability.[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post-text),[#SamAltman](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/samaltman&trk=public_post-text),[Google](https://www.linkedin.com/company/google?trk=public_post-text),[Sundar Pichai](https://www.linkedin.com/in/sundarpichai?trk=public_post-text),[Meta](https://www.linkedin.com/company/meta?trk=public_post-text)[#MarkZuckerberg](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/markzuckerberg&trk=public_post-text),[Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post-text)[Satya Nadella](https://www.linkedin.com/in/satyanadella?trk=public_post-text),[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/linkedin?trk=public_post-text)[Ryan Roslansky](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ryanroslansky?trk=public_post-text), this one is for you. Before talking about the future of AI, please fix the present state of your customer experience. Because if this is what “intelligent systems” look like, the real intelligence lies in knowing when not to believe the hype.

Shopify CEO email to managers: We are not a family

Team,
Leadership is tough. Leadership through times of crisis and ambiguity is doubly tough. Leadership through times of multiple compounding global tidal waves can seem impossible. To refer back to my Summit talk, Shopify is in a new box that we don’t understand yet. The world is in a new box that it barely understands yet. We’ve only mapped out a small corner of this box and have just started exploring the rest of the vast dark patches. It will take some time.
What’s more, our team members need us more than ever. The best thing we can do for them is not add to the ambiguity. Shopify hasn’t historically been great at setting clear expectations across the organization and I think this is starting to cause an enormous amount of managerial debt that’s ballooning out of control.
I can’t tell you how to do that in your various departments. But a good start would be to remind everyone that we are a business. More importantly, we are a hugely ambitious one. We are trying to create a world class product that gives superpowers to the merchants that we are obsessed over. Everything Shopify does is to accomplish this, and everyone at Shopify should be able to describe how their job, through a series of direct or indirect steps, furthers this mission.
To help you make this more clear to your team members, here are some pointers about what Shopify is not:
Shopify, like any other for-profit company, is not a family. The very idea is preposterous. You are born into a family. You never choose it, and they can’t un-family you. It should be massively obvious that Shopify is not a family but I see people, even leaders, casually use terms like “Shopifam” which will cause the members of our teams (especially junior ones that have never worked anywhere else) to get the wrong impression. The dangers of “family thinking” are that it becomes incredibly hard to let poor performers go. Shopify is a team, not a family.
We literally only want the best people in the world. The reason why you joined Shopify is because – I hope – all the other people you met during the interview process were really smart, caring, and committed. This is magic and it creates a virtuous magnetism on talented people because very few people in the world have this in themselves. People who don’t should not be part of this team. This magic and magnetism is a product of tight performance management that I expect all of us to get back to.
Shopify is also not the government. We cannot solve every societal problem here. We are part of an ecosystem, of economies, of culture, and of actual countries. We also can’t take care of all your needs. We will try our best to take care of the ones that ensure you can support our mission. Shopify’s worldview is well documented – we believe in liberal values and equality of opportunity. Sometimes we see opportunities to help nudge these causes forward. We do this because this directly helps our business and our merchants and not because of some moralistic overreach.
We want to build one of the best companies in the world. We obsess about our merchants. We want everyone to have a shot at bettering their lot through entrepreneurship. We want to make and keep Shopify, the product, world class or die trying.
Only way to do this is through having incredible people. Some of them we hire on future potential, and we help them but expect them to grow into their potential. Some of them we bring in further down their careers. But we all have to re-qualify for our jobs every year. The red-queen race of Shopify’s historic 40% or better growth is that everyone has to show up at least 40% better every year to qualify for our current jobs. I expect you to hold yourself and your teams to this standard. Judge this improvement based on having a growth mindset, deepening the craft, taking risks, making better decisions, and doing what it takes to better support our mission and our merchants.
We will always have compassion for team members in truly difficult situations. For example, those who find themselves suddenly becoming primary caregivers or those who are struggling with mental health issues. There are also second chances, especially for those who have been top performers before. Outside of those cases we need to remind everyone that like any other competitive (sports) team, it matters how you show up every day and contribute to the team’s success. Beyond straight performance output, everyone that engages in endless Slack trolling, victimhood thinking, us-vs-them divisiveness, and zero sum thinking must be seen for the threat they are: they break teams. Teams survive and thrive on the actions of the collective, and the cohesiveness of the whole. Poor performance and divisiveness cannot be tolerated.
If this sounds at all surprising, this is because we somehow lost something. Shopify has always been like this. I feel that a lot of these core beliefs have been muddied over recent years. So in my capacity as the one person who has witnessed every minute of Shopify’s existence, I want to reiterate some of these core principles. Shopify is as successful as it is right now precisely because of the downstream effects of those early ideas. Currently we are successful despite the muddying. This will not work for much longer. Let’s get back there.
Despite all the external buzz around Shopify (market cap, biggest company in Canada, …) we are still really early. We are in the big leagues amongst the biggest and baddest companies in the world. When we succeed in our mission, millions of merchants do better. Millions of people find employment. We have the opportunity to make that tens and even hundreds of millions in the future. I’m here for this potential, and I need you to be here for that too.
—
OK, that’s a lot to take in. You might be tempted to take what’s up there and run it through some kind of lowpass filter and translate it into your own language before discussing it with your peers and your leads. Don’t. Above is what I need everyone to understand. It’s important not to muddy a message that fights against the muddying of principles. You are responsible for reinforcing these lessons and holding your teams accountable to them. The Talent team will follow up with next steps in the coming days. Even better, actively help them with ideas and opportunities to implement those ideas. This is what leadership in action looks like.
– tobi

Do you know the appropriate tools to use for your Product-Led Growth (PLG)? Part III We've talked about the differentiated strategy. Next up is the Disruptive growth strategy. A disruptive strategy helps you serve already over-served customers. What you are doing basically is charging less for a product that performs less. Canva's strategy is one. Although Canva gets the job done easily, its quality can never be compared to Adobe Photoshop. Coursera vs. traditional universities is another example. The freemium model is the best disruptive strategy. Here are the questions to ask when using the disruptive strategy: ✔️ Is your market full of over-served customers? ✔️ Are you competing in a hyper-competitive market? ✔️ Do you have the resources to support a freemium model? ✔️ Can your onboarding be completely self-service? So, to make a decision on your growth strategy, ask yourself if your SaaS product follows a dominant, differentiated, or disruptive approach. ❓ Do you want to offer the best solution for the lowest price (Dominant Strategy) ❓ Do you want to offer the best-customized solution for the highest price to underserved customers? (Differentiated Strategy) ❓ Do you want to offer the simplest product for the lowest price to over-served customers? (Disruptive Strategy) ❓ Or are you planning on using a hybrid strategy? This ends the series on the tools for PLG

Lily Grozeva

Google’s AI Mode is quietly rewriting how B2B visibility works. Kevin Indig’s new usability study of AI Mode (link to the Growth Memo edition in comments) confirms what many of us suspected. ‼️ User behavior has shifted far more radically than most brands realize. • 88% of users focus on the AI-generated text first. • Clicks to external sites are close to zero in informational queries. • Inline links (within the text) outperform citation icons by 27%. • And the single strongest influence on what users trust or buy? Brand familiarity. This matters because in B2B tech, we’ve built visibility frameworks on a model that’s disappearing, where organic search drove discovery, evaluation, and conversion through content journeys. In AI Mode, those journeys now happen inside the SERP. Your content may never get the click. Your brand will. That means two strategic shifts for tech marketers: 1. From keywords to entities. AI Mode surfaces “trusted sources,” not optimized pages. If your brand isn’t seen as authoritative in its category, you’re not even in the conversation. E-E-A-T signals and entity consolidation are becoming the real distribution levers. 2. From traffic to trust. Measuring success by CTR or sessions will soon look archaic. The new KPI is in-SERP visibility, how often your brand appears (and is quoted) inside AI Mode responses. Think of it as brand-level share of voice across machine-generated outputs. The takeaway for B2B growth leaders is uncomfortable but clear: ‼️ Stop fighting for page-one rankings that no longer drive the behavior you’re optimizing for. Start investing in the brand authority that determines whether AI Mode quotes, cites, or ignores you. AI Mode kills weak brands. Don't be that brand.

Brands that connect AI directly to first-party data will win — because shoppers won’t just see related products, they’ll see relevant ones. 2️⃣ Offline → online identity linking One of the biggest challenges in commerce is still connecting the in-store shopper to their online profile. Tech like Brij is helping close that loop — turning anonymous retail buyers into trackable, loyal customers across both channels. It’s the key to creating a seamless brand experience (and finally understanding your full customer journey!) 3️⃣ Generative AI for product development From CPG innovation to packaging design, generative AI is collapsing feedback loops and bringing products to market 10x faster. We’re entering an era where data-driven creativity is the differentiator. Brands that embrace AI-assisted innovation will outpace those still stuck in manual iteration cycles, so pay attention! 4️⃣ Omnichannel loyalty + data ownership With privacy regulations tightening, owning your customer relationship is everything. We’re seeing more brands build their own loyalty ecosystems and rely less on third-party data. If you’re not already developing a first-party data strategy this quarter, you’re behind the curve. Get on it! 5️⃣ AI assistants for founders + operators AI tools are evolving from “helpers” to decision copilots. They’re starting to manage meeting recaps, customer insights, even hiring workflows. I use AI Co-Pilots ALL the time so I can focus on high-impact strategy instead of outsourceable admin work. Which of these are you already experimenting with, and which ones do you need some more info on? 👇🏼

AI memo from the CEO of Shopify

` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `
Reflexive AI usage is now a baseline expectation at Shopify
> Context: This is a Shopify internal memo that I shared here because it was in the process of being leaked and (presumably) shown in bad faith
Team,
We are entering a time where more merchants and entrepreneurs could be created than any other in history. We often talk about bringing down the complexity curve to allow more people to choose this as a career. Each step along the entrepreneurial path is rife with decisions requiring skill, judgement and knowledge. Having AI alongside the journey and increasingly doing not just the consultation, but also doing the work for our merchants is a mind-blowing step function change here.
Our task here at Shopify is to make our software unquestionably the best canvas on which to develop the best businesses of the future. We do this by keeping everyone cutting edge and bringing all the best tools to bear so our merchants can be more successful than they themselves used to imagine. For that we need to be absolutely ahead.
Reflexive AI usage is now a baseline expectation at Shopify
Maybe you are already there and find this memo puzzling. In that case you already use AI as a thought partner, deep researcher, critic, tutor, or pair programmer. I use it all the time, but even I feel I'm only scratching the surface. It’s the most rapid shift to how work is done that I’ve seen in my career and I’ve been pretty clear about my enthusiasm for it: you've heard me talk about AI in weekly videos, podcasts, town halls, and… Summit! Last summer I used agents to create my talk, and presented about that. I did this as a call to action and invitation for everyone to tinker with AI, to dispel any skepticism or confusion that this matters at all levels. Many of you took up the call, and all of us who did have been in absolute awe of the new capabilities and tools that AI can deliver to augment our skills, crafts, and fill in our gaps.
What we have learned so far is that using AI well is a skill that needs to be carefully learned by… using it a lot. It’s just too unlike everything else. The call to tinker with it was the right one, but it was too much of a suggestion. This is what I want to change here today. We also learned that, as opposed to most tools, AI acts as a multiplier. We are all lucky to work with some amazing colleagues, the kind who contribute 10X of what was previously thought possible. It’s my favorite thing about this company. And what’s even more amazing is that, for the first time, we see the tools become 10X themselves. I’ve seen many of these people approach implausible tasks, ones we wouldn’t even have chosen to tackle before, with reflexive and brilliant usage of AI to get 100X the work done.
In my On Leadership memo years ago, I described Shopify as a red queen race based on the Alice in Wonderland story—you have to keep running just to stay still. In a company growing 20-40% year over year, you must improve by at least that every year just to re-qualify. This goes for me as well as everyone else.

AI Is No Longer Optional Noah Treshnell 11 months ago
The "Leak" Edition Marco Andre 12 months ago
Agentic AI Just Went Mainstream: The Week That Changed… Metizsoft Solutions Private Limited 1 week ago
This sounds daunting, but given the nature of the tools, this doesn’t even sound terribly ambitious to me anymore. It’s also exactly the kind of environment that our top performers tell us they want. Learning together, surrounded by people who also are on their own journey of personal growth and working on worthwhile, meaningful, and hard problems is precisely the environment Shopify was created to provide. This represents both an opportunity and a requirement, deeply connected to our core values of Be a Constant Learner and Thrive on Change. These aren't just aspirational phrases—they're fundamental expectations that come with being a part of this world-class team. This is what we founders wanted, and this is what we built.

1. Using AI effectively is now a fundamental expectation of everyone at Shopify. It's a tool of all trades today, and will only grow in importance. Frankly, I don't think it's feasible to opt out of learning the skill of applying AI in your craft; you are welcome to try, but I want to be honest I cannot see this working out today, and definitely not tomorrow. Stagnation is almost certain, and stagnation is slow-motion failure. If you're not climbing, you're sliding.
2. AI must be part of your GSD Prototype phase. The prototype phase of any GSD project should be dominated by AI exploration. Prototypes are meant for learning and creating information. AI dramatically accelerates this process. You can learn to produce something that other team mates can look at, use, and reason about in a fraction of the time it used to take.
3. We will add AI usage questions to our performance and peer review questionnaire. Learning to use AI well is an unobvious skill. My sense is that a lot of people give up after writing a prompt and not getting the ideal thing back immediately. Learning to prompt and load context is important, and getting peers to provide feedback on how this is going will be valuable.
4. Learning is self directed, but share what you learned. You have access to as much of the cutting edge AI tools as possible. There is chat.shopify.io which we had for years now. Developers have proxy, Copilot, Cursor, Claude code, all pre-tooled and ready to go. We’ll learn and adapt together as a team. We’ll be sharing Ws (and Ls!) with each other as we experiment with new AI capabilities, and we’ll dedicate time to AI integration in our monthly business reviews and product development cycles. Slack and Vault have lots of places where people share prompts that they developed, like#revenue -ai-use-cases and#ai -centaurs.
5. Before asking for more Headcount and resources, teams must demonstrate why they cannot get what they want done using AI. What would this area look like if autonomous AI agents were already part of the team? This question can lead to really fun discussions and projects.
6. Everyone means everyone. This applies to all of us—including me and the executive team.

AI will totally change Shopify, our work, and the rest of our lives. We're all in on this! I couldn't think of a better place to be part of this truly unprecedented change than being here. You don't just get a front-row seat, but are surrounded by a whole company learning and pushing things forward together.
Our job is to figure out what entrepreneurship looks like in a world where AI is universally available. And I intend for us to do the best possible job of that, and to do that I need everyone’s help. I already laid out a lot of the AI projects in the themes this year- our roadmap is clear, and our product will better match our mission. What we need to succeed is our collective sum total skill and ambition at applying our craft, multiplied by AI, for the benefit of our merchants.
-tobi CEO Shopify

Last month, Shopify’s CEO Tobias Lütke made waves with a bold AI memo… | Greg Shove

````````````[Skip to main content](#main-content)

[![View profile for Greg Shove]()](https://www.linkedin.com/in/gregshove?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Greg Shove](https://www.linkedin.com/in/gregshove?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
CEO @ Section | founder @ Machine and Partners | new essays every week @ personalmath.substack.com
2wEdited
Last month, Shopify’s CEO[Tobias Lütke](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/tobiaslutke?trk=public_post-text)made waves with a bold AI memo about the company’s AI-first future. But what struck me most wasn't what he said - it was what he didn’t.
Right now, most executives are either acting as AI cheerleaders - or not saying much at all.
They’re talking about AI in terms of "transformation" and "productivity." About how AI will enhance teams, not replace them.
Tobi, to his credit, took it a step further, with specific expectations around AI use - including that AI proficiency will be included in employee performance reviews.
But what he didn’t say is that he wants the company to keep growing, but with less headcount. His AI vision means getting to $10B in annual revenue, but with 6,000 employees - not the 8,000 that work there today. That part was not covered in his memo - and I get that. Hard for a public company CEO to pre-announce layoffs. Not much upside in that - and besides, the audience for that message -  investors - can read between the lines. It’s not easy to talk about the impact of AI on our teams - but we know enough now to say something, as our employees are thinking and talking about it every day. AI won’t hit every team the same way. But it will hit every team. The way I see it, there are three likely outcomes for each team - and this is what I have told my team at Section:
1. Some teams will shrink.
This is the hardest truth. AI will let us do the same work with fewer people.
No one wants to say it, but it’s already happening.
2. Some teams will grow.
These are the high-ROI teams where AI supercharges output.
Sales is the clearest example. If AI lets one rep close more deals, then I want more reps, not fewer. Same for software engineers, since my roadmap is always behind schedule.
3. Most teams will stay the same - but do more.
The same headcount, but higher output.
This is where AI will enable more output without visibly changing the team size.
And on many teams, there will likely be new jobs added or re-purposed, as we add AI-native roles.
Oh, and one last thing: I just bought Shopify stock for the first time ever.
Not because of what Tobi said. Because of what he didn’t.

Shopify's CEO says "Using AI effectively is a fundamental expectation" of employees, there will be AI usage questions in performance reviews, and they must demonstrate why they can't use AI when asking for more headcount or resources. Some of the things Tobi Lutke shared in an internal memo to all staff: "[we] have been in absolute awe of the new capabilities and tools that AI can deliver to augment our skills, crafts, and fill in our gaps." "We also learned that, as opposed to most tools, AI acts as a multiplier." "Using AI well is a skill that needs to be carefully learned by... using it a lot." "Frankly, I don't think it's feasible to opt out of learning the skill of applying AI in your craft; you are welcome to try, but I want to be honest I cannot see this working out today, and definitely not tomorrow." "We are all lucky to work with some amazing colleagues, the kind who contribute 10X of what was previously thought possible... I’ve seen many of these people approach implausible tasks, ones we wouldn’t even have chosen to tackle before, with reflexive and brilliant usage of AI to get 100X the work done." How does this communication about AI compared to your company? "AI must be part of your GSD (Get Shit Done) Prototype phase. The prototype phase of any GSD project should be dominated by AI exploration. Prototypes are meant for learning and creating information. AI dramatically accelerates this process." "Learning is self-directed, but share what you learned... Slack and Vault have lots of places where people share prompts that they developed"

"AI is a tool. The choice about how it gets deployed is ours." Artificial Intelligence is revolutionizing the way we do business. For beginners stepping into this evolving landscape, the notion that AI is just a tool - waiting to be wielded by human hands - is both empowering and a call to action. Shopify is a 10,000 person company worth $150B, and Tobi Lütke still runs… | Dan Hockenmaier | 188 comments

````````````[Skip to main content](#main-content)

[![View profile for Dan Hockenmaier]()](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dan-hock?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)
[Dan Hockenmaier](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dan-hock?trk=public_post_feed-actor-name)
CSO at Faire; danhock.com
2moEdited
Shopify is a 10,000 person company worth $150B, and Tobi Lütke still runs it like a video game:
- There is no annual or quarterly plan
- He reviews the entire roadmap with each team every 6 weeks
- To power this meeting, they built a custom tool that collects information on every project happening across the company. It includes what is being built and how the engineers are building it - the literal API calls
- Teams can’t bring anything except what is in the tool to the meeting - no framing, no data, no presentations
- Based on these meetings Tobi decides what to keep and what to kill
- He then allocates engineers to each team. The rest of headcount planning is done in a GitHub repo based on ratios with engineers
It's rare to find companies breaking the "best practices" of org design. But if you look at just the companies that are leading in the highest growth categories - the best of the best - they are less rare than many people think. More examples (from Apple, Airbnb, Nvidia) in the full essay:[https://lnkd.in/g9MuevAj](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/g9MuevAj&urlhash=M4Sy&trk=public_post-text)

Bridging Business, Technology & Human Experience | Executive Advisor | AI Ethicist | Business Transformation | Devoted Husband and Father of 8
4w
[Shopify](https://ca.linkedin.com/company/shopify?trk=public_post-text)CEO[Tobias Lütke](https://ca.linkedin.com/in/tobiaslutke?trk=public_post-text)’s latest announcement about their bold move to mandate AI use as a requirement for all employees, backing it by pointing to how they’re changing their hiring, incentive, and accountability systems, is an indicator of where things are going.
I have no doubts this makes a lot of people uncomfortable. Nobody likes to be told what to do, especially from the top.
And, this is just the tip of the iceberg. Shopify is just the beginning. I know countless senior leadership teams with plans to follow suit who have been waiting to pull the trigger to avoid being the first.
Now, to be clear, that doesn’t mean “Adopt AI or perish.” However, if you choose to stay in the anti-AI camp, you have to recognize and accept that you are choosing a path that will have an entirely new set of hurdles. It’s neither wrong nor more noble; it’s just different.
However, whether you choose to adopt AI or flee from it, you will be forced to choose your path and accept the challenges that come with either one.

How does AI optimize and improve the way you do work so that you're able to do things more effectively? And if you're like, well, I, I don't really. There's a good chance that's going to cost you the job and it will only cost you more jobs as we move forward with where the current job market is right now. This is not a mistake you want to make. You don't want to say, well, I'm not really familiar with it. I don't really know how to use it. I I'm not really sure where it fits into my work that will work against you. And like I said. Can it become a competitive advantage? Yes, but no, it's a harder road. And an example I'll give with that just it's unrelated to AI is I for a long time have known that travel unnecessary travel like you know, some people are just fine with traveling or whatever for work or relocating. It's not something I was going to do for a job so. Right. Now, did that make finding a job in the past harder? Of course it did. Of course it did. But it was a conscious, intentional decision. And so whenever it came up in an interview, I was fully prepared for how I had already thought that through, why I had built in, you know, structure around it to make sure that that wasn't a limitation. If anything, it was a catalyst for my success. Like I was prepared for it. I wasn't like, ohh, well, no, I don't do that. That that would have cost me countless. I never would have got a C-Suite job that was fully remote with no travel. If I had not put that intentionality into it.

As a cognitive psychologist who's spent 15 years navigating the intersection of tech and human experience, I couldn't agree more that the future of work is already being written - we're just not ready for it yet. What's interesting to me is how this shift will impact our decision-making processes, particularly when faced with new technologies like AI. Will we rely on intuition and expertise, or seek additional knowledge and training? How will we communicate effectively about the benefits and risks of AI adoption? As someone who's worked closely with business leaders, I believe it's essential to prioritize a nuanced understanding of human-AI collaboration and its implications for organizational success.

Tobi Lutke explains what the VCs who passed on Shopify got wrong 🤯Tobi recounts pitching Shopify to VCs on Sand Hill Road a few years after founding Shopify.
Investors passed because they thought the addressable market was too small. At the time, there were about 40,000-50,000 online stores, and even if Shopify captured 50% of the market, that still wouldn’t be a venture-scale business.
When Tobi ran into the VC partner a few years ago, the partner asked Tobi what he missed (Shopify is valued at almost $100 billion today).
Tobi explained:
“You were actually correct, but what you didn’t realize was that Shopify was the solution to the very problem you identified. The reason there was only 40,000 online stores was because it was hard, expensive, and everyone who tried ran into all these brick walls of complexity, which Shopify, one after another, smoothed over and made simple to do.”
Tobi believes this is a common mistake:
“What a lot of free-market thinkers don’t understand is that between the demand and eventual supply lies friction. And I actually think that friction is probably the most potent force for shaping the planet that people just generally do not acknowledge… That was my theory when I turned my snowboard store into Shopify: there was a lot more people like me except there was too much friction which we needed to solve. And Shopify has proven out that every time we make the process simpler, there’s more consumption. At this point, we have a million merchants on Shopify, which is a mind-blowing number. So friction is a major component, and it’s something that software is uniquely good at reducing.”
Video source: Startup Archive

Four Things I Loved About Tobi Lütke’s AI Memo
Shopify CEO Tobi Lütke recently shared an internal memo on how his company is embracing AI. It echoed many of my own beliefs on modern leadership and what it takes to fuel AI adoption at scale. A few ideas especially stood out to me—because they’re relevant for every company, not just digital natives.  1) He sets a vision, even without all the answers. Too many CEOs wait until the solutions are perfectly clear before moving. Innovative CEOs who create differential value for customers are willing to set a direction of travel, without knowing all the details of the “how.”
2) He is leading from the top. At Bain, we strongly believe this is a CEO-led business transformation moment. Lütke describes how he is tinkering with AI tools, role modeling the desired behavior for the organization. And he holds himself and his team accountable: “Everyone means everyone. This applies to all of us—including me and the executive team.”
3) He emphasizes key areas of focus as well as broad citizen innovation. It’s a both/and approach—one that is top-down and bottom-up. Lütke requires that the prototype phase of all Shopify “GSD projects” must incorporate AI exploration. He also notes that learning is self-directed, encouraging employees to share wins and loses across monthly business reviews and internal communication channels.  4) He puts employees at the center. Lütke is setting clear expectations for employees and empowering them to act in line with the direction of the organization. He is bringing everyone along—not just specialists, engineers, or IT. The memo sets the precedent that all employees, at all levels of the company, should be learning how to use AI tools, together.  Yes, Shopify is a modern tech company. But these principles apply to every organization. So, what expectations have you set for your employees?[https://lnkd.in/g8f4W7CW](https://lnkd.in/g8f4W7CW?trk=public_post-text)

Shopify CEO demands proof AI can't do a job before hiring | Karel Vredenburg posted on the topic | LinkedIn

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Karel Vredenburg
11mo Edited
Shopify CEO Tobias Lütke recently told employees they must prove that AI can’t do a job before requesting to hire a new person. It’s a striking example of how rapidly AI is reshaping the workforce—and how employers are recalibrating their assumptions about human contributions. Read the article here: https://lnkd.in/gysbr5TK Read the memo to staff here: https://lnkd.in/gE86A6tc I love the power of AI but as I shared in a previous post, over 220 million people on LinkedIn—21% of all users—have marked themselves as “Open to Work.” That number has grown by 35% in just the past year. This isn’t just a hiring trend. It’s a disturbing warning sign. As AI displaces roles faster than new ones are created, we need policy innovation to keep up with technological innovation. Humans being replaced by AI once paid taxes. AI workers don’t. That’s why several leaders have proposed a robot tax: companies that replace human workers with AI would pay into a fund to support those displaced. If a machine is doing the work, the benefits shouldn’t only flow to the company is the reasoning. As automation surges and the number of “open to work” professionals grows, it’s time for business leaders, policymakers, and society to ask: Who benefits from AI—and who pays the price? I’d like to commend Shopify CEO Tobi Lütke for embracing AI—though, in my view, taking it too far—and for being transparent with his team about the shift, as well as open in sharing his memo. At the same time, I’d welcome his thoughts on this: Should companies like his that replace human employees with AI contribute the equivalent of those employees’ tax contributions into a fund to support those displaced by the technology? In an earlier post, I suggested we start a movement using our design and research skills—to help shape a more humane and just world, one that values employees as much as shareholders. AI replacing human workers is another topic we’ll be addressing. Thanks to those who’ve already reached out to join. DM me if you’d like to be part of this movement too.#shopify#AI#layoffs#jobloss#unemployed#opentowork#robottax

About 264,000 jobs were cut in 2023, another 152,000 to 238,000 in 2024, and almost 100,000 already in 2025, which averages to nearly 500 jobs lost every single day. At least 27,000 of those are directly tied to generative AI and another 20,000 to automation. AI evolves faster than training cycles, and firms are flattening their structures, not expanding them. Microsoft saved $500 million by cutting 9,000; Amazon’s CEO tied AI to “less jobs”; IBM replaced 94% of HR with AI. “Learn AI” doesn’t fix a system rigged to minimize human labor, not reward it. Unemployment has long been seen as structural or cyclical, but today we face something I will call: "𝑴𝒂𝒄𝒉𝒊𝒏𝒆-𝑭𝒊𝒓𝒔𝒕 𝑼𝒏𝒆𝒎𝒑𝒍𝒐𝒚𝒎𝒆𝒏𝒕"; a category where human labor becomes economically irrelevant because companies believe machines can do the work cheaper, faster, and without limits. This is not about skill gaps or recessions; traditional remedies won’t fix it. If we fail to recognize it as unprecedented, we will keep chasing shadows while the fabric of society unravels. The consequences spill far beyond the office. Consumer spending has dropped, mental health has declined, property crime is rising, substance abuse is up by double digits, divorce and domestic violence rates have climbed. The fallout will cost society billions of dollars a year in healthcare, policing, and social services etc. To ensure we are building for all, here are a few recommendations I could think of: giving workers a voice in boardrooms, increasing transparency around AI’s impact on jobs, ensuring access to retraining and fair pay, treating data as a public resource, strengthening regulations to guide responsible AI adoption, auditing algorithms for bias and fairness, setting ethical standards for AI deployment, and creating independent oversight to monitor workplace impacts.

If you are the CTO of an e-commerce company expanding internationally that is running on headless commerce and need to hire 5–10 React engineers fast, here’s the playbook you want to run to fill the positions within 6 weeks: 1. Clearly define the skills and qualities you're seeking in candidates. If you ask candidates the same interview questions as everyone else, you have no filter, you have no advantage and you have no ideas! The ideal recruit for an e-commerce developer role is: • Technical, • an artist, • constantly generating new knowledge, and • using AI to automate the repetitive parts of their job. (Exceptions apply.) So, how do you filter for that? 2. Track record. Look for candidates who have done the job in the past. • Do they know Next.js inside out? • Did they craft pixel-perfect designs? • Did they work with marketing stakeholders and can they translate technical jargon into easily understandable user requirements? • Have they ever implemented A/B testing solutions & optimized SEO to do CRO? Ask candidates why they prefer working for e-commerce companies. You want people who are obsessed about optimizing every click, every impression, and are comfortable putting in extra hours to get the job done. Where do you find people like that? 3. Tap into your network. A-players are recruited through high-trust channels. Ask yourself, who do you know who has a proven track record in your domain or has the connection to veterans in the e-commerce industry? Simultaneously, tap into your peer group of other founders, CTOs, and trusted alumni you've worked with in the past. Finding these people takes time. What do you do in the meantime to maintain momentum? 4. Bridge the gap. Leverage specialized agencies that provide developers focused on your technology. This allows you to ramp up your team immediately, and then slowly off-ramp the contract roles as you find the perfect full-time hires. This strategy minimizes your opportunity cost, and the fixed rates make your budget planning simple and predictable. As CTO for an e-commerce company, your time is your only truly finite resource. Every site that is unoptimized, every feature that is missing is costing you $$$.

Specialists are out. Generalists are in. That's the shift I'm seeing in eCommerce hiring right now. For years, everyone was told to focus on a niche. "Pick a platform and stick to it" "Be the best at the one thing you do." "Focus on development, don't bother with marketing." Even I've given that advice over the years. And companies want leaner teams with each person wearing many hats. This year alone, I've placed: IT leaders who are also eCommerce strategists Shopify devs running CRO and SEO eCom managers who also do motion design And the trend isn't stopping: Front-end developers who are also designers. DevOps Engineers who can own QA entirely Marketers owning email, paid media, content generation, and more. The market's not rewarding narrow expertise anymore. It's rewarding range. I favor the "T-shaped" analogy - build deep expertise in one or two areas and build breadth everywhere else. Are you seeing this trend as well, or will Specialists win the day yet?

"Prove that AI can’t do the job before asking for more headcount.”
That’s what Shopify CEO Tobi Lütke told his team recently - and honestly, it makes sense.
In a world where AI tools are becoming more capable by the day, this shifts the mindset from defaulting to hiring →to defaulting to optimization 🤖📉.
And it raises important questions for all of us:
◾Are we truly using AI in our workflows before we ask for more resources?
◾Have we built a culture that supports experimentation with tools like ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, or others?
◾Or are we still treating AI like a side project instead of a core capability?

Lately, I often find myself reflecting on how artificial intelligence is transforming the way we work. We’re living through a new revolution — after the industrial one, mass digitalization, and the rise of e-commerce. Like every revolution, it brings opportunities but also disruption. Some professions will radically change, others will disappear, and entirely new roles will emerge — roles we can’t even imagine today. I’m not sure whether this will be the final revolution — the one that replaces humanity’s last bastion: our intellect, creativity, adaptability, and ability to imagine new solutions. But one thing is certain: factory workers still exist, even after the industrial revolution; accountants still exist, even after spreadsheets; physical stores still exist, even after e-commerce. They’ve changed, they’ve evolved. And I believe the same will happen with artificial intelligence. The real risk isn’t AI itself — it’s standing still while everything else changes. Those who learn to reinterpret their professional value, by integrating or differentiating from AI, won’t just survive — they’ll thrive.

There’s this common assumption that AI will widen the gap between big enterprises and everyone else. I think the opposite will happen. AI doesn’t just scale. It levels the playing field. A small business that can automate data entry, analyze customer insights, and personalize outreach at scale suddenly operates like a company ten times its size. The difference isn’t access anymore. It’s execution. Big companies have resources, sure. But they also have layers of approvals, committees, and roadblocks that slow them down. Small businesses? They can make a decision on Monday and be live by Friday. That’s where the real advantage lies. When an SMB adopts AI intentionally — starting small, measuring fast, iterating often — they can move with a level of speed that most large enterprises only dream about. And that’s why I believe the future of AI isn’t enterprise-driven. It’s SMB-powered. Because the businesses that are closest to their customers, quickest to adapt, and most willing to test? They’re the ones who will win. At Neferdata, our mission is to make that future happen sooner by giving small teams the same leverage, accuracy, and automation once reserved for companies with entire IT departments. AI doesn’t belong to the biggest players anymore. It belongs to the fastest adopters.

AI Is Simple. Adoption Isn’t. I see this all the time. Companies pour everything into technology, thinking it’s the hard part. It’s not. AI is already brilliant. 🎯 When AI fails in an SMB context Here’s a scenario you’ll recognize if you’re in the SMB world: A U.S.-based e-commerce company (~30 people) launched an AI chatbot to automate customer support. The idea was elegant: reduce headcount, speed replies, improve experience. What went wrong: • Their customer data was fragmented across silos, spreadsheets, old systems. • Many questions required nuance and judgment that the bot couldn’t handle reliably. • Negative user feedback piled up — the bot’s answers frustrated, not helped. • The business had no clear KPIs to measure success — when things slipped, there was no clear “go back” trigger. Ultimately, they pulled the plug and reverted to human agents. This isn’t rare — it’s almost the rule in SMB AI launches. ✅ If you’re leading AI adoption in an SMB, remember: 1. Start with one narrow, well-understood use case. 2. Clean, unified data is non-negotiable. 3. Set your KPIs first. 4. Expect iteration — don’t expect perfection out of the gate. 5. Always maintain a human fallback. AI isn’t magic. It’s amplification — when your foundation is weak, you simply amplify problems. 🙏 Let AI run the process. You run the vision. 🙏#SMB#AI#DigitalTransformation#Leadership#TechStrategy

Think back to the late ’90s— Pets.com was everywhere. 🐾 Their sock puppet mascot was on TV, they ran Super Bowl ads, magazines raved about their “explosive growth,” and investors couldn’t hand over money fast enough. Lately, I’ve been having a lot of conversations with moms who want to start a business but feel stuck. Mostly, it’s because they think they need a big launch or a perfect plan first. I’ve been there too. And after starting several small ventures (some that worked, some that didn’t), I’ve learned this: real progress usually looks quiet. It’s not a major launch or a big announcement. It’s small, scrappy steps no one sees — and that’s okay. Taking action on your dream business doesn’t have to mean pulling off a major launch. I don’t believe in doing grand launches when you don’t even have an audience yet. Here’s what you can do instead: Start small — really small. Make a quick mockup. Create a simple post or brief about your idea. Send a message to someone who might need it. Offer to help one person and see what happens. Learn from it. Repeat. That’s how you build something that works — by starting where you are, with what you have, and testing it in real life (not in your head). You don’t need the perfect offer, logo, or funnel to start. You just need to show up once today. Test one idea. Sell one version. Momentum doesn’t come from big launches. It comes from quiet, consistent action — the kind that fits your real life. That’s what I call a Scrappy Starter.

Tobi Lutke’s AI Memo: A Blueprint for the Post-Software Era at Shopify

Tobi Lutke’s AI Memo: A Blueprint for the Post-Software Era at Shopify
In early April 2025, Shopify CEO Tobi Lutke released a bold internal memo that immediately drew attention across the tech and business world. Titled “AI usage is now a baseline expectation,” the memo wasn’t just a note to employees - it was a clear signal that Shopify is doubling down on AI as a fundamental layer of its business operations. Lütke, known for his product-first leadership style and early bets on digital commerce infrastructure, laid out a direct challenge: adapt to AI or risk becoming obsolete within the company.
This memo arrives at a time when AI is no longer viewed as a “tool” but a foundational capability - similar to how cloud computing or mobile development once were. What Lutke articulates is not just a shift in tooling, but a systemic redefinition of how work gets done at Shopify, from individual contributor roles to strategic decision-making.
---

AI isn’t a tool - it’s a new operating system. The memo makes it clear: Shopify doesn’t just see AI as another tool in the tech stack. It sees AI as a new operating system for the company - one that governs not only how software is written but how decisions are made, resources are allocated, and value is created.
The goal is leverage, not just productivity. Where many organizations are still focused on AI as a productivity enhancer (ie, “write faster emails” or “summarize this document”), Shopify is shifting the conversation toward leverage. By forcing teams to consider whether AI can do the job before hiring, Shopify is aiming to build a smaller, faster, more efficient company that can scale without bloating.
Shopify wants to be an AI-native company before it’s a competitive necessity. Lutke’s move is preemptive. The AI transformation of business is inevitable, but most companies are slow to act. Shopify’s memo reads like a first-mover manifesto - a chance to build the company’s second act on a foundation of agility, automation, and algorithmic thinking.
A cultural test for tech workers. For Shopify employees, this memo is a wake-up call. AI fluency will soon be a baseline expectation in hiring, promotion, and daily collaboration. Lutke is effectively telling his team: we are changing the rules of the game - and you need to change with them.
---

Tobi Lutke’s AI directive is likely to be seen in retrospect as a turning point - not just for Shopify, but for how large tech companies integrate and operationalize AI across every layer of the business. While many companies are still cautiously experimenting with AI pilots, Shopify is treating AI like electricity: assumed, everywhere, and essential.
This memo joins a growing wave of similar leadership signals from other forward-thinking CEOs - including Satya Nadella at Microsoft, Brian Chesky at Airbnb, and Jensen Huang at Nvidia - all pushing their organizations to think AI-first, not just AI-aware.
But Lutke’s directive is perhaps the clearest articulation yet of what that actually means inside a tech company. It also sets a precedent: moving forward, any startup or scaled tech company that doesn’t encourage (or mandate) this level of AI fluency may start to look outdated.

Venture funding activity remained robust over the past week, marked by several high-profile rounds across the US, Europe, and Asia. AI and enterprise SaaS continued to draw investor appetite, while fintech and biotech also saw notable traction. Key funding highlights include:
OpenAI – the San Francisco-based AI juggernaut – reportedly secured a massive $40B investment led by SoftBank, valuing the company at $260B. If confirmed, this would mark one of the largest private investments ever in a tech company, reinforcing OpenAI's dominance in the generative AI arms race.
Isomorphic Labs – an AI-first drug discovery platform based in the UK – raised $600M in a new round led by Thrive Capital. Backed by Google DeepMind, the company uses AI models to predict protein structures and accelerate pharmaceutical R&D timelines.
Solace – a healthcare navigation platform – closed a $60M Series B round led by Menlo Ventures. The company connects patients with trained advocates to help them manage complex healthcare experiences. With Medicare recently expanding reimbursement for advocacy services, Solace is well-positioned for rapid scale.
Scapia – an India-based travel and fintech platform – raised $40M Series B led by Peak XV Partners. The startup offers a travel-focused credit card and app targeted at younger consumers. The funding will support new product rollouts and team expansion.
Inn-Flow – a US-based back-office SaaS platform for hotels – secured $45M in growth equity from Mainsail Partners. The platform handles accounting, labor management, and business intelligence for thousands of hospitality clients, aiming to modernize hotel operations globally.
Pennylane – a French accounting and finance automation platform – raised €75M in fresh funding as it continues its push to become the go-to solution for European SMEs and accountants. The company hit unicorn status in 2024 and is doubling down on product development.
Axelera AI – a Netherlands-based AI chip startup – was awarded a €61.6M grant from the EuroHPC DARE project to accelerate development of its Titania chip for generative AI and computer vision applications. The funding highlights Europe’s deepening investment in AI infrastructure.
PyannoteAI – a Paris-based voice tech startup – raised $9M in a seed round led by Crane Ventures and Serena. The company specializes in “speaker diarization,” a technology that distinguishes individual voices in a conversation—an increasingly vital tool for transcription, meetings, and AI-human interaction.
Sagittal AI – a UK-based developer productivity startup – secured £2.2M in pre-seed funding to build “AI coworkers” that integrate seamlessly into dev workflows. Early interest from enterprise customers and VCs signals momentum for AI-native workplace tools.
---

Strategic consolidation is picking up across the startup landscape, with venture-backed companies becoming both acquirers and attractive targets. From fintech to enterprise tech and consumer brands, startups are leveraging M&A to scale faster, unlock new markets, and broaden their product offerings.
One standout move came from UK-based specialist insurance startup Icen Risk, which sold a 49% stake to Zurich Insurance Group in a deal that reportedly values the company at £150 million. Icen provides risk coverage for M&A transactions - think tax liabilities and IP risks - and Zurich’s backing will supercharge its global distribution, especially across North America and Europe. The deal is a clear signal that incumbents are opting to buy innovation rather than build it.
In enterprise tech, Enabled Analytics, a Texas-based data infrastructure startup, merged with Ventas Consulting to strengthen its position in the digital transformation space. The combination brings deeper analytics capabilities and expands Enabled’s access to Fortune 1000 clients, representing a classic scale-up play in a highly competitive B2B software market.
On the consumer front, LesserEvil, a health-focused snack startup best known for its popcorn, was acquired by Hershey in a $750 million deal. The move allows Hershey to fast-track its entry into the “better-for-you” snacking category - an area growing far faster than traditional confectionery. For LesserEvil, the exit offers massive distribution and resources to compete with global brands.
In aviation-adjacent tech, regional airline startups Republic Airways and Mesa Air Group announced an all-stock merger, creating one of the largest regional carriers in the U.S. While not pure tech companies, both have made sizable investments in mobility innovation and flight operations software - making this one of the more ambitious operational mergers in the travel-tech ecosystem.

Tobias Lütke is possibly the most real, inspiring, and first-principled thinkers I've ever met. He also operates Shopify very differently from other companies at this scale: — The product roadmap is… | Lenny Rachitsky | 43 comments

1y Edited
Tobias Lütke is possibly the most real, inspiring, and first-principled thinkers I've ever met. He also operates Shopify very differently from other companies at this scale: — The product roadmap is driven by intuition, taste, and a 100-year vision — KPIs are banned on their core product teams — They're fully remote (and plan to stay that way) — They randomly delete all recurring meetings — He encourages teams to approach *every* problem from first-principles In our conversation, we discuss: 🔸 What Tobi believes it takes to be a great product leader 🔸 How Tobi specifically tackles problems from first principles 🔸 Why Tobi works backward from a 100-year vision 🔸 How he leveraged his unique talent stack 🔸 The importance of focusing on joy and delight 🔸 Why complexity kills entrepreneurship 🔸 The power of following your curiosity 🔸 Much more Listen now 👇 • YouTube: https://lnkd.in/gHxrQkNC• Spotify: https://lnkd.in/gDYXEEN6• Apple: https://lnkd.in/gP_Ndcug Thank you to our wonderful sponsors for supporting the podcast: 🏆 Sinch— Build messaging, email, and calling into your product: https://sinch.com/lenny🏆 Liveblocks— Ready-made collaborative features to drop into your product: https://lnkd.in/gX2FjvbQ🏆 Loom— The easiest screen recorder you’ll ever use: http://loom.com/lenny Some key takeaways: 1. Everyone has more potential than they realize. Don’t shy away from providing tough feedback that challenges them to aim higher. As leaders, it’s our job to help others see the best version of themselves, even if it means pushing them outside their comfort zone. The best growth happens when you’re constantly nudging yourself and your team to do better than they thought they could. 2. Work backward from a long time horizon: a. A 100-year perspective helps optimize for lasting impact over short-term gain b. Playing positive-sum games with customers becomes the ultimate growth strategy c. Long-term thinking enables better partnership decisions and helps resist value extraction 3. Just as founders inject energy into their startups, you need to find what drives your passion and energy. Is it dissatisfaction with the status quo? Is it a rivalry, or a desire to build something better? Understanding your source of energy can help you remain motivated through challenges and lead your business with conviction. 4. You need both a positional game (long-term strategy) and tactics (short-term wins). Too many focus only on tactics—getting the immediate conversion or winning the next A/B test. But true growth comes from setting yourself up for success in the long run, shaping your company’s role in the ecosystem, and building trust over time.

All startup founders struggle with positioning: learning that the right focus creates faster growth, and discovering where to focus. However, bootstrapped founders usually create on a high-leverage positioning sooner than VC-funded founders. Anthony Pierri and Robert Kaminski are the co-founders of FletchPMM, a positioning strategy firm for B2B SaaS companies. They started Fletch after observing that most founders often confuse positioning with copywriting and marketing, and built their business by helping SaaS leaders make the tough, strategic choices about who they serve and how they win. Over the last three years, Anthony and Rob have worked with more than 400 SaaS companies, from early-stage startups to scaling-up companies. Fletch helps founders and B2B SaaS leaders clarify their positioning, sharpen their messaging, and translate strategy into effective homepage design. In our discussion this week on the Practical Founders Podcast, they help founders see that positioning IS business strategy, not copywriting. It forces clarity and tradeoffs to improve execution. Positioning is the clarity you have around these important questions: - Who are we really for, specifically? Who do we not market and sell to? - How do we describe what we offer to the world? - What benefit do we actually deliver to our target customer? - How are we different from the competition and alternatives? This clarity and focus make any execution work better. Anthony and Rob are on LinkedIn every week, reminding us why founders must (eventually) choose one clear path to scale efficiently.

Most founders think once you’ve raised millions, the hard part is over. But if you’re building in a traditional industry, that’s where the real work begins. This week on Funded, Now What?!, I sat down with Mark Miller Founder & CEO of Insurevision.ai, a UK-based startup using AI and what he calls Telematics 2.0 to reinvent how insurance companies understand driver behavior. The Great Human Rebrand: Advancement, or Just Better Buzzwords? You ever notice how every generation thinks it’s reinventing progress — but really, we’re just renaming the same things and raising the subscription fee? We used to build pyramids. Now we “scale startups.” Same structure, same sweat, just better coffee and worse sleep. We used to gather around the fire to share stories. Now it’s a podcast. Same warmth, fewer stars, more microphones. “Farming” became “bio-sustainability.” “Talking” became “stakeholder alignment.” “Resting” became “strategic recovery between sprints.” We don’t evolve, we iterate. We trademark our habits and call it innovation. We even put KPIs on kindness and OKRs on creativity. Still craving meaning. Still pretending the next update will fix it. Maybe advancement isn’t about running faster, or calling the wheel a “Continuous Improvement Framework.” Maybe it’s about realizing that sometimes, the most advanced move... is to stop spinning. Progress isn’t what we name it. It’s what we notice when we finally pause long enough to breathe. “Funny how every new ‘methodology’ ends up feeling like the old one, just with more slides and better fonts.” 😄 Does anyone relate to this?
